coredata.dc:title: Ion exchange membrane optimized light-driven photoelectrochemical unit for efficiency simultaneous organic degradation and metal recovery from the mine wastewater

coredata.dc:description: Resource recovery from wastewater is a promising and challenging topic. Herein, a well-designed ion exchange membrane optimized light-driven photoelectrochemical unit (MPECS) was constructed to reduce the effect of inorganic salt on the photoelectrochemical performance of the photoanode. TiO2/carbon dots/WO3 (TCDW) photoanode with the indirect Z-scheme heterojunction structure was successfully fabricated, achieving a strong light harvest performance (10.82%) and a high photocurrent density (5.39 mA/cm2). For the simulated solution (0.01 M phenol and 0.01 M CuSO4), the phenol degradation and Cu recovery efficiencies reached 99.67% and 62.20% in 60 min, respectively, and the corresponding photoelectric conversion efficiency (PECE) reached 4.64% in the TCDW/Pt-based MPECS. For the actual Cu-laden mine wastewater, over 98% of inorganic salt was removed. Compared to the traditional photoelectrochemical system (PECS), the COD removal and Cu recovery efficiencies were further improved by 23.77% and 49.41% in MPECS, respectively. The results exhibited a promising light-driven mine wastewater treatment technology.

Variations of the light response potential on the (d) Q235-b cathode, (e) 304SS cathode, and (f) Pt cathode surfaces.

Reaction conditions: [phenol]= 0.01 M; [Cu2+]= 0.01 M, solution volume= 300 mL, flow rate: 10 mL/min, light intensity: 100 mW/cm2, effective photoanode working area: 1.0 cm2, the inorganic salt removal unit volume: 1 L, pH: 6.27–6.73.

surface curves of the photoanodes, EPR results of the photoanodes (b) in the photocatalysis system without connecting Pt cathode, and (c) in the MPECS connecting with the Pt cathode; the energy band alignment and the photogenerated electron transfer pathways of (d) TW, and (e) TCDW.

Evaluation of the MPECS performance during treating actual Cu-laden mine wastewater. (a) Variations of the ion concentrations before and after PECS and MPECS treatments, insert plot: optical photos of the wastewater before and after treatment in PECS and MPECS; (b) Organic degradation and metal ion recovery mechanisms of MPECS for treating actual Cu-laden mine wastewater; (c) key parameters of PECS and MPECS during the practical wastewater treatment. Reaction conditions: the effective TCDW area: 1.0 cm2, flow rate: 10 mL/min, light intensity: 100 mW/cm2.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resource recovery from wastewater is a promising and challenging topic. Herein, a well-designed ion exchange membrane optimized light-driven photoelectrochemical unit (MPECS) was constructed to reduce the effect of inorganic salt on the photoelectrochemical performance of the photoanode. TiO2/carbon dots/WO3 (TCDW) photoanode with the indirect Z-scheme heterojunction structure was successfully fabricated, achieving a strong light harvest performance (10.82%) and a high photocurrent density (5.39 mA/cm2). For the simulated solution (0.01 M phenol and 0.01 M CuSO4), the phenol degradation and Cu recovery efficiencies reached 99.67% and 62.20% in 60 min, respectively, and the corresponding photoelectric conversion efficiency (PECE) reached 4.64% in the TCDW/Pt-based MPECS. For the actual Cu-laden mine wastewater, over 98% of inorganic salt was removed. Compared to the traditional photoelectrochemical system (PECS), the COD removal and Cu recovery efficiencies were further improved by 23.77% and 49.41% in MPECS, respectively. The results exhibited a promising light-driven mine wastewater treatment technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Water quality deterioration and resource shortage have become two major obstacles impeding economic development (Cui et al., 2018b; Sun et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Mine wastewater as a pollution source contains large amounts of metal resources that can be recovered via the reduction process (Li et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2022). For the photocatalysis technology, the photo-generated electron can be used as the electron donor to achieve metal resource recovery, such as Ag+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, etc. (Shi et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a). Meanwhile, the generated holes contribute to the generation of high-activity radicals to degrade organic pollutants (Zong et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020). However, unsatisfactory photoelectric conversion efficiency (PECE) limits the development of the photocatalysis technology.

TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) are widely used as photoresponsive material owing to its strong photoelectrochemical stability (Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). To further improve the photoelectrochemical performance, various materials have been applied to modify TNAs by constructing heterojunction structures. According to the theoretical energy band alignment, a type II heterojunction structure can be constructed by decorating TNAs (E
CB : −0.29 VRHE and E
VB : 2.91 VRHE) with WO3 (E
CB : 0.74 VRHE and E
VB : 3.44 VRHE) (Bayrak-Pehlivan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018, 2019; Zhang et al., 2021b). Photo-generated electrons and holes are theoretically gathered at E
CB (WO3) and E
VB (TNAs), respectively. However, there is room for further enhancement of the separation efficiency. Carbon dots (CDs) with strong electron-transfer capability are well recognized as the electron-transfer mediator (Wu et al., 2021a; Yu et al., 2016), serving as the bridge to accelerate the transfer of the photo-generated electron from the E
CB (WO3) to the E
VB (TNAs). Finally, the photogenerated electrons and holes are theoretically gathered at the E
CB (TNAs) and the E
VB (WO3), respectively. Compared to the TNAs/WO3 (TW) nanocomposite, the recombination potential barrier of the electron-hole pairs is enlarged in the ternary TNAs/CDs/WO3 (TCDW) nanocomposites.

Another effective method to boost PECE is to construct a photoelectrochemical system (PECS) by connecting a metallic cathode with the photoanode, strengthening the directional movement of the photo-generated electron (Xiao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020b). However, some interfering substances coexist with these target resources, such as SO4 2-, Cl-, NO3 -, etc. These inorganic ions can adsorb on the photoanode surface, blocking the active site and reducing the light harvest capability. Meanwhile, inorganic salt accelerates the breakdown of the passive film on the cathode surface, shortening the lifetime of the metallic cathode and reducing the stability of PECS (Li et al., 2021b). It is generally accepted that the breakdown of the passive film due to the substance interchange between the metallic cathode and the medium, which can be accelerated by inorganic anions (SO4 2- and Cl-). Therefore, it is of great significance to reduce the effect of inorganic salts in terms of maintaining the high efficiency and stable operation of PECS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the photoanodes and construction of the MPECS
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical agents were directly used without further purification (Note S1). TNAs were synthesized via the anode oxidation method; carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using the H2SO4 acid-assisted bottom-up method; TNAs/CDs (TCD) and TW were synthesized via the dipping and the electrodeposition methods, respectively (Note S2). The detailed information on the physicochemical property and the photoelectrochemical activity tests are described in Note S3.

A schematic illustration of MPECS is shown in Fig. 1a. The MPECS was mainly composed of the photocatalytic reaction unit (2), the metal recovery unit (6), and the inorganic salt removal unit (4). The feedwater was firstly pumped into the reactor from the inlet of the metal recovery unit (7), and then, the feedwater flowed into the photocatalytic reaction unit from the outlet of the metal recovery unit (8) to the inlet of the photocatalytic reaction unit (9). Then, the solution flowed from the outlet of the photocatalytic reaction unit (10) to the inlet of the metal recovery unit (7). Based on the above flow direction, a complete circulation path was constructed. In addition, deionized (DI) water was pumped into the inorganic salt removal unit from the inlet (11) and flowed out from the outlet (12). Cations and anions entered into the inorganic salt removal unit by passing through the cation exchange membrane (CEM) and anion exchange membrane (AEM), respectively. Processes Ⅰ, Ⅱ, and Ⅲ represent the light harvest process, the photo-generated electron transfer process, and the photo-generated electron utilization process, respectively.

Detailed information on the cathode is described in Note S4. In this work, total of 12 groups were designed to evaluate the MPECS performance, including four kinds of photoanodes (TNAs, TCD, TW, and TCDW) and three kinds of cathodes (Q235-b carbon steel, 304 stainless steel, and Pt foil), connecting by a copper wire (5 cm, 0.1 Ω). A Xe lamp source (HSX-F300, NBeT, China) with 100 mW·cm−2 light intensity was used as the driving force.

Evaluation of the photoelectrochemical performance: The current and potential signals between the photoanode and the cathode were recorded using an electrochemical workstation in zero galvanometer mode (PGSTAT204, Metrohm, Switzerland), which was used to calculate ETE. The transient current and potential signals on the cathode surface were recorded under the intermittent light irradiation, which was used to evaluate the photoresponse characteristics of the cathode. The cathode, Pt, and Ag/AgCl were used as the WE, CE, and RE, respectively (Fig. S2).

Evaluation of the water purification performance: The experiments were conducted in a 300 mL simulated solution containing 0.01 M phenol (0.9411 g/L) and 0.01 M CuSO4.5 H2O (2.4969 g/L). The flow rate and the simulated solar light intensity were set as 10 mL/min and 100 mW/m2, respectively. The experiment was initiated under light irradiation, and then, a 1.5 mL liquid sample was withdrawn every 5 min at the outlet of the photocatalytic reaction unit to measure the concentrations of Cu2+ and phenol. In consideration of the pollutant structure, some other refractory organic pollutants (benzoic acid, bisphenol A, carbamazepine, perfluorooctanoic acid, and triclosan) were also applied to evaluate the degradation performance of the MPECS. The measurement methods can be seen in Note S5. Finally, actual Cu-laden mine wastewater was collected from Baise (China), which was used to evaluate the MPECS performance during the actual Cu-laden mine wastewater treatment.

Typical well dispersed CQDs morphology with a diameter in the range of 1–7 nm was observed ( Fig. 2a). According to the lattice fringe, the interplanar distance was 0.21 nm, identified as the (100) plane of CQDs (Zhou et al., 2020). Other characterization results further confirmed the successful fabrication of CQDs, revealing abundant functional groups and unsaturated bonds, a long quantum lifetime (3.59 ns), and strong fluorescence characteristics (Note S6). Highly ordered nanotube arrays were grown on the Ti foil surface, and the inner diameter was mainly distributed in the range of 60–100 nm (Fig. 2b). After the dipping process, a homogeneous CDs layer was formed on the TNA surface (Figs. S5a and S5b). CDs and TNAs were well connected owing to the existence of 3-mercaptopropionic acid that contained carboxylate (-COOH) and thiol (-SH) functional groups (Kishimoto et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). TNAs and CDs facilitated to connect with 3-mercaptopropionic acid via -SH and -COOH, respectively. Therefore, Ti, O, and C were detected as the main elements on the TCD surface. After the electrodeposition process, a homogeneous WO3 nanoparticle layer was deposited on the TNAs and TCD surfaces, forming TW (Figs. S5c and S5d) and TCDW (Fig. 2c), respectively. Meanwhile, W was detected as one of the main elements on the TW and TCDW surfaces, confirmed by the EDS results. However, after the dipping process, CQDs were aggregated, forming CDs with a diameter over 20 nm (Fig. 2d and Fig. S5e).

The characteristic diffraction peaks of the nanotube were well matched to the monoclinic phase of anatase TiO2, indicating that the nanotube arrays consisted of anatase TiO2 (Fig. 2e). Compared to TNAs, the diffraction peaks of TCD were well matched to TNAs but with a broad amorphous natural peak in the region of 15°~35°, revealing that CDs were successfully decorated on the TNAs surface without changing the anatase TiO2 crystal planes (Luo et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Apart from the anatase TiO2 diffraction peaks, new monoclinic phase WO3 diffraction peaks appeared in TW and TCDW, indicating that the WO3 nanoparticle layer was successfully deposited on the TNAs and TCD surfaces. Similarly, no significant shift was observed in TW and TCDW, demonstrating that the anatase TiO2 structure was stable during the electrodeposition processes.

For the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra, TCD exhibited the highest C peak intensity among all photoanodes, both TW and TCDW exhibited significant W peaks, further confirming the successful decoration of CDs and WO3 on the TNAs surface (Fig. 2 f). The Ti 2p high-resolution spectra could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 464.7 eV and 458.9 eV, which were assigned to the Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 peaks, respectively (Fig. 2 g). The binding energy between the two peak centers was close to 5.8 eV with respect to the symmetric Ti 2p spectrum shape, revealing that four valence states (TiO2) of the Ti atom were the main chemical states. However, a slight peak shift was observed in both TW and TCDW. This shift represented the variations in the Ti valence state, resulting from the electron exchange between TiO2 and WO3 during the electrodeposition process. For the C 1 s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2 h), three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.3 eV were split and assigned to the C-C, C-O, and O-CO chemical bonds in TCD, respectively (Alqadami et al., 2020). Two peaks at 285.4 eV and 286.5 eV were divided in TCDW, which were classified as the C-O and C-O-C peaks, respectively (Wei et al., 2018). The results indicated that the unsaturation degree of CDs was decreased after the WO3 electrodeposition processes. Both TW and TCDW curves could be deconvoluted into two major peaks at 35.7 eV (W 4 f7/2) and 37.8 eV (W 4 f5/2), indicating the existence of the six valence states of W (WO3) (Fig. 2i) (Jiang et al., 2018). Other XPS results further confirmed the successful decorating of CDs and WO3 (Note S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical performance of the photoanodes and MPECS
ce:section.ce:para:
TNAs exhibited typical anatase TiO2 light absorption characteristics, exhibiting a stronger light adsorption capability in the ultraviolet light region than the visible light region ( Fig. 3a). The absorption edge was near 388 nm and the corresponding bandgap (E
g ) was 3.20 eV, calculating by Eq. (S3). After decorating CDs, the light adsorption performance was significantly enhanced due to the strong light-harvesting capability of CDs (Gharat et al., 2019). Compared to TNAs, the absorption edges were shifted to 439 nm and 422 nm for TW (E
g : 2.82 eV) and TCDW (E
g : 2.93 eV), respectively. According to the Tauc plot (Fig. 3b), the E
g values of TNAs, TW, and TCDW were 3.20 eV, 2.82 eV, and 2.90 eV, respectively, which were similar to the ultraviolet-visible absorption diffuser reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) results. The energy band alignment results were further confirmed by the valance band XPS curves and Mott-Schottky results (Note S8).

The steady photocurrent densities of TW, TCD, and TCDW were approximately 1.54, 2.48, and 5.91 mA/cm2, respectively, which were 2.49, 4.01, and 9.55 times than those of the pristine TNAs (0.62 mA/cm2) (Fig. 3c). It should be noted that the increment of the TCDW photocurrent density was higher than the summed increments of TCD and TW, exhibiting a good synergetic effect of CDs and WO3. However, a sharp transient spike was obtained for all photoanodes at the beginning, and then, the photocurrent density gradually tended to stabilize. For TCDW, a high separation efficiency was achieved, and large amounts of holes accumulated at E
VB in the beginning. Then, the accumulated holes were gradually recombined with the photo-generated electrons until the balance between the generation rate and recombination rate was achieved (Yu et al., 2014).

The following factors contributed to increasing the photocurrent density. On the one hand, TW and TCDW exhibited narrower E
g values than TNAs, contributing to enhancing LHE. On the other hand, previous studies have reported that the facets of WO3 could spatially self-separate electron-hole pairs. Holes preferred to gather at the (002) facets (peak at 23.07° in Fig. 2e), while electrons were preferentially dominated at the (200) (peak at 24.32° in Fig. 2e) and (020) (peak at 23.59° in Fig. 2e) facets (Zhang et al., 2014). This pre-separation process facilitated suppressing the recombination of the separated electrons and holes, resulting in a high photocurrent density. The above analysis results contributed to enhance the LHE of the photoanode following TCDW (10.82%) > TCD (6.42%) > TW (3.18%) > TNAs (1.46%) (Fig. 3d).

The maximum applied bias photon to current efficiency (ABPE) of TNAs, TCD, TW, and TCDW was 0.24% (0.34 VRHE), 0.42% (0.62 VRHE), 0.46% (0.64 VRHE), and 0.52% (0.62 VRHE) calculating by Eq. (6), respectively (Fig. 3e). TCDW exhibited the highest ABPE among all photoanodes, representing the potential best PECE. The results were strongly consistent with the other electrochemical test results (Note S9) (Zhou et al., 2020).
(6)
ABPE =
J × ( 1.23 − U )
I
light
× 100
where, J (mA/cm2) and U (VRHE) are the photocurrent density and the applied bias, respectively. All of the current densities gradually increased with increasing potential. Compared with the pristine TNAs (2.33 mA/cm2), the current densities increased to 3.19, 4.91, and 5.39 mA/cm2 for TCD, TW, and TCDW at a bias of 1.23 VAg/AgCl, respectively (Fig. 3f). The results indicated that TCDW exhibited the strongest electron response performance among all photoanodes.

It is well recognized that photo-generated electrons can be transferred from the photoanode to the cathode surface, resulting in the variations of the current and potential response signals on the cathode surface (Windle et al., 2019), which were recorded to evaluate the photoelectrochemical performance of MPECS ( Fig. 4). TCDW exhibited the best photoelectrochemical activities among all photoanodes, resulting in the good photoelectrochemical performance of MPECS. The stable photoresponse currents were < 0.5 μA/cm2, 0.29 mA/cm2, and 0.81 mA/cm2 for Q235-b, 304SS, and Pt cathodes coupled with TCDW, respectively (Fig. 4a~c). Meanwhile, the variations in the light response potentials were < 0.01 mVAg/AgCl, 0.06 mVAg/AgCl, and 0.17 mVAg/AgCl for the Q235-b, 304SS, and Pt cathodes coupled with TCDW, respectively (Fig. 4d~f). The corresponding enlarged images can be seen in Figs. S12~S15. In MPECS, the cathode was used as the electron acceptor to boost the directional transfer process of the photo-generated electron. All cathodes exhibited good photoresponse signals, except for the Q235-b cathode. Unsatisfactory photoresponse performance of the Q235-b cathode is due to its low E
OCP , hindering the photogenerated electron transfer process. The ETE was in the range of 54.01~58.99% in the Pt-based MPECS, which was higher than that of Q235-b and 304SS cathodes (Fig. S17). The results indicated that the photo-generated electrons preferentially transferred from the TCDW photoanode to the Pt cathode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the water purification and metal recovery performances
ce:section.ce:para: The phenol degradation (organic pollutant) and Cu recovery (heavy metal) performances of PECS and MPECS are shown in Fig. 5. The phenol degradation efficiency was in the order TNAs (16.82%) < TCD (24.59%) < TW (34.18%) < TCDW (37.87%) in PECS (Fig. 5a). After constructing an inorganic salt removal unit, the phenol degradation efficiency was significantly increased to 42.06% (TNAs) < 76.83% (TCD) < 85.46% (TW) < 99.67% (TCDW) in MPECS (Fig. 5b). Meanwhile, the observed reaction constant (k) was increased from 7.40 × 10−3 min−1 (TCDW/Pt-based PECS) to 9.36 × 10−2 min−1 (TCDW/Pt-based MPECS) (Table S2). For the metal recovery unit, the Cu recovery efficiency was following: TNAs (15.50%) < TCD (20.44%) < TW (26.26%) < TCDW (31.10%) in PECS (Fig. 5e). In contrast, the Cu recovery efficiency was increased to 31.00% (TNAs) < 41.08% (TCD) < 45.25% (TW) < 62.20% (TCDW) in MPECS (Fig. 5 f). Meanwhile, the k was increased from 6.25 × 10−3 min−1 (TCDW/Pt-based PECS) to 7.97 × 10−2 min−1 (TCDW/Pt-based MPECS) (Table S2).

TCDW exhibited the best organic pollutant degradation performance among all of the photoanodes, followed by TCD and TW. According to the surface charge injection efficiency (E
surface ) ( Fig. 6a, S20, and S21), more separated holes could reach the photoanode surface in TCDW than other photoanodes. Combined with the LHE results (Fig. 3d), we believe that more photogenerated holes were generated and utilized in TCDW than other photoanodes, resulting in a rapid degradation rate and good degradation performance for organic pollutants. In addition, some refectory organic matters with chain structure (perfluorooctanoic acid) and polycyclic structures (carbamazepine, triclosan, bisphenol A) were selected to evaluate the organic degradation performance of MPECS. All selected organic pollutants can be degraded in 60 min (Fig. S22). The results indicated that TCDW exhibited good organic degradation performance in MPECS. It should be noted that good Cu recovery performances (>90%, 1.05 × 10−2~5.61 × 10−2 min−1) were observed for the Q235-b cathode (Table S2 and Figs. S18 and S19). However, barely any photo-generated electrons transferred from the photoanode to the Q235-b cathode surface, as confirmed by the photoelectrochemical performances (Fig. 4). Therefore, the good Cu recovery performance of Q235-b cathode derived from the replacement reaction of Fe and Cu2+, reaching 88.85% (Fig. S23). In contrast, the Cu recovery capability of the 304SS cathode was derived from the photogenerated electrons rather than the replacement reaction.

A layer of crimson powder can be observed on the cathode surfaces after 60 min operation (Fig. S24), which were observed using electron microscopy and analysed using energy spectrum. The FESEM-EDS and TEM-mapping results revealed that the crimson powders mainly consisted of Cu and CuxOy (Fig. S25). After removing the surface Cu/CuxOy layer, a significant corrosion phenomenon was observed on the Q235-b surface rather than the 304SS surface (Fig. S26). Two conclusions could be inferred from the observation results: one conclusion is that the Cu recovery process of the Q235-b cathode was derived from the replacement reaction rather than the photo-generated electron transfer process; another conclusion is that the transferred photo-generated could be used as the electron donor to protect cathode from corrosion, maintaining the operation stability and the cathode lifetime (Cui et al., 2018a).

It should be noted that both phenol degradation and Cu recovery performances were significantly increased after constructing an inorganic salt removal unit. The conductivity was gradually increased from 25 μs/cm to 220 μs/cm in the inorganic salt removal unit (Fig. S27), exhibiting good ion removal performance. According to the MPECS structure, SO4 2- passed through the AEM driven by the differential ion concentrations between the inorganic salt removal unit and the metal recovery unit. Therefore, the effect of SO4 2- on poisoning the photoanode was reduced, resulting in the exposure of a large number of active sites on the photoanode surface. The exposed active sites facilitated the enhancement of LHE, contributing to improving the organic pollutant degradation efficiency. Meanwhile, the higher utilization efficiency of the photogenerated hole contributed to the higher ETE, resulting in a good Cu recovery performance. The ε
F of Pt cathode-based MPECS reached 80.31% (TNAs), 90.62% (TCD), 86.48% (TW), and 90.78% (TCDW), exhibiting high photogenerated electron utilization efficiencies (Fig. S29). Moreover, the organic pollutant degradation performance was relatively stable during the cycling test, with only a slight decrease after the 10th cycling run (Fig. S30). In contrast, the Cu recovery performance gradually decreased with increasing operation time, which might have resulted from the increase in heat loss during the photogenerated electron transfer process. Compared to the pristine TCDW, the characteristic diffraction peaks in XRD pattern (Fig. S31) and the photocurrent density (Fig. S32) were almost the same after 10 cycling runs. The results indicated that the inorganic salt removal unit could effectively avoid the photoelectrochemical performance of the photoanode. Therefore, MPECS exhibited better water purification and Cu recovery performances than PECS under the same conditions. Compared to the previous literatures (Table S3 and S4), MPECS exhibited good simultaneous organic pollutant degradation and Cu recovery performances.

Combined with the energy band alignment and the photoelectrochemical activity test results, an interesting result was obtained. Normally, a higher LHE resulted from a narrower E
g . However, TW (2.82 eV) exhibited a narrower E
g than TCDW (2.90 eV) (Fig. 3b) but with a lower photocurrent density (Fig. 3c). We believe that the opposite result was derived from the different electron transfer pathways. Our expectation is to modify the traditional type II heterojunction structure (TW) into the Z-scheme heterojunction structure (TCDW). Therefore, the relationships among the energy band alignment, electron transfer pathway, water purification, and Cu recovery performances were analysed.

According to the EPR results, typical DMPO-OH peaks (hydroxyl radicals, •OH) in the prompt formation of a quartet (1:2:2:1) and weak DMPO-O2 peaks (superoxide radical, •O2 -) in the prompt formation of a triplet (1:1:1) were detected in TNAs, TCD, and TCDW without a connecting cathode (Wu et al., 2021b; Zhang et al., 2021a). In contrast, only DMPO-OH peaks were detected in TW (Fig. 6b). It is well recognized that the positions of O2/•O2 - and H2O/•OH were near − 0.13 VRHE and 2.38 VRHE, respectively. •O2 - and •OH could be generated under the condition of a low E
CB (<−0.13 VRHE) and a high E
VB (>2.38 VRHE), respectively. For TNAs, E
CB (−0.29 VRHE) was lower than O2/•O2 - and E
VB (2.91 VRHE) was higher than H2O/•OH. Therefore, both •OH and •O2 - could be detected. For TCD, the energy band structure barely changed after the dipping process but with a high LHE owing to the strong LHE of CDs. Therefore, both •OH and •O2 - could still be detected, and the phenol degradation performance was enhanced. For TW, electrons, and holes were gathered in the E
CB (WO3, 0.74 VRHE) and E
VB (TNAs, 2.91 VRHE) owing to the type Ⅱ heterojunction structure, respectively (Fig. 6d), but only with •OH peaks. In addition, electrons and holes were gathered on the different semiconductors, facilitating the suppression of recombination. Therefore, stronger organic degradation performances were obtained in TW than in TNAs and TCD.

For TCDW, electrons and holes were gathered in the E
CB (TNAs, −0.29 VRHE) and E
VB (WO3, 3.44 VRHE) owing to the Z-scheme heterojunction structure (Fig. 6e). Therefore, both •OH and •O2 - were detected. Moreover, E
g of TCDW was larger than that of TW, representing a potentially good separation efficiency of the electron-hole pair. Therefore, TCDW exhibited the best phenol degradation performance among all photoanodes. In contrast, only significant DMPO-OH peaks were detected for all photoanodes connected to the Pt cathode (Fig. 6c). The results indicated that the photogenerated electrons were directionally transferred from the photoanode to the Pt cathode rather than the generation of •O2 - in MPECS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MPECS for treating practical mineral wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Practical copper mine wastewater was collected and used to evaluate the MPECS performance. After 3 h of operation, the COD concentration was decreased from 1376 mg/L to 1084 mg/L (21.22%) in PECS after 3 h of operation. Compared to PECS, the COD degradation efficiency was increased from 21.22% to 44.99% in MPECS ( Fig. 7a), revealing that the refractory organic pollutant degradation capability was enhanced after constructing an inorganic salt removal unit. Meanwhile, the copper mineral wastewater chroma was significantly decreased from dark brown to light yellow owing to the oxidization of the chromogenic functional groups (insert plot of Fig. 7a). However, compared to the degradation performance for the simulated solution, the degradation efficiency was decreased from 100% to 44.99%, which was owing to the following two factors: On the one hand, the high chroma of the practical wastewater reduced the light harvest performance of TCDW. On the other hand, practical wastewater contained some refectory organic matter that had a more complicated structure than phenol. Some degradation intermediate products still contributed to COD, which was not taken into account during phenol degradation. However, the SO4 2- and NO3 - removal efficiencies reached 98.37% and 98.39% by the inorganic salt removal unit, contributing to the good COD removal efficiency.

For the metal ions recovery, various metal ions coexist with Cu2+, including Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, As3+, and Fe3+. The Cu concentration decreased from 22.63 mg/L to 19.44 mg/L and 8.26 mg/L for PECS (14.09%) and MPECS (63.50%), respectively, and the Cu recovery efficiency increased over four times. According to the Cu recovery efficiency, slight effect of the coexisting ions on the Cu recovery was obtained. However, the recovery weight of Cu was significantly decreased in the simulated solution (62.2% of 635.5 mg/L) than the actual Cu-laden mine wastewater (63.5% of 22.63 mg/L) owing to the competition of the coexisting cations. According to the ion concentration results, the K+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, As3+, Mn2+, and Zn2+ concentrations were decreased. In addition, compared to the simulated experiment, the operation time was increased to 3 h, and more photo-generated electrons were generated and utilized to recover Cu.

For both PECS and MPECS, LHE is the key parameter to evaluate the effect of chroma on the photoanode, and the LHE of TCDW still reached 5.61% in a practical wastewater environment (Fig. 7c). The ETEs of the PECS and MPECS was 38.12% and 79.36%, respectively. The high ETE of MPECS was derived from the removal of inorganic ions. The corresponding ε
F values in terms of the Cu recovery were 28.09% and 69.84% in PECS and MPECS, respectively, which were slightly lower than the results for treating the simulated solution. According to Eq. (4), the PECE was 3.11% in MPECS, which was mainly affected by the LHE rather than the ETE and ε
F . Therefore, MPECS exhibited good simultaneous water purification and metal recovery, performances in a practical wastewater environment.

Normally, Fe exposed on the Q235-b and 304SS surfaces, resulting in the unsatisfied operation stability of the MPECS. According to ε
F , nearly 70% transferred photogenerated electrons were used to recover Cu. The rest of the photo-generated electrons were consumed to protect cathode from the inorganic anion ion attack (Supplementary Fig. S25a), ensuring the stability of MPECS. According to null pair theory, oxygen vacancies (V
O
s) and Fe vacancies (V
Fe ) were simultaneously generated at the interface between the cathode and the solution. V
Fe moved towards the cathode surface and immediately combined with Fe, forming Fe
Fe and generating metal vacancies (V
m ). Fe
Fe as the carrier brought Fe towards the solution, resulting in the loss of Fe and the instability of the cathode. However, the generated V
m was combined with Fe and accumulated on the cathode surface, facilitating the annihilation of V
m . The amount of annihilated V
m in a unit area was considered the metal vacancy annihilation flux (J
m ). Meanwhile, the generated V
O moved towards the solution and combined with SO4 2- instead of oxygen due to the strong binding capability of SO4 2-, forming V
A . Subsequently, V
A could react with Fe
Fe , resulting in the formation of V
Fe and V
O . The amount of the generated V
Fe in a unit area is defined as the cation vacancy flux (J
cv ). Fe
Fe could react with V
A , generating a large amount of V
Fe and contributing to an increase in J
cv . Normally, if J
cv > J
m , V
Metal would accumulate on the cathode surface, instability phenomenon preferentially occurred at these V
Fe enrichment regions, including the breakdown of the passive film and the corrosion of the cathode, and then, the operation stability of MPECS was decreased.

After connecting with the photoanode, the photogenerated electrons were transferred from the photoanode to the cathode surface under light excitation. The amount of transferred electrons in a unit area is defined as the electron generating flux (J
g ), and the amount of annihilated electrons in a unit area is defined as the electron annihilating flux (J
a ). Photogenerated electrons were easily combined with SO4 2- rather than V
O owing to their strong reduction capability. Then, the generation processes of V
O and V
Fe were suppressed, avoiding the loss of Fe (Fig. S25b). If J
g > J
a , the amount of the transferred photogenerated electron as the electron donor could effectively ensure the stability of the cathode. If J
a > J
g , SO4 2- would combine with V
O , accelerating the mass transfer process. According to the ε
F results, the transferred photogenerated electrons were mainly used to recover Cu2+ rather than maintain the stability of MPECS, indicating J
g > J
a . Therefore, the TCDW photoanode could effectively ensure the stability of the cathode and MPECS (Geringer and Macdonald, 2012; Macdonald, 2011; Obot et al., 2015; Sharifi-Asl et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012).

In summary, a light-driven MPECS wastewater treatment unit was constructed and a high-efficiency indirect Z-scheme TCDW photoanode was fabricated for simultaneous water purification and metal resource recovery. The results exhibited a promising light-driven Cu-laden mine wastewater treatment technology. The main results can be concluded as follows.
(a)
The photoelectrochemical activity of TNAs was significantly improved after decorating CDs and WO3, contributing by the narrowed E
g (2.90 eV) and the strong LHE (10.82%). Photocurrent density, as one of the key parameters, was increased near an order of magnitude (9.55 times).
(b)
For the TCDW/Pt-based MPECS, satisfied ETE (58.99%), good ε
F (90.78%), and high PECE (4.64%) were achieved, facilitating the high phenol degradation (99.67%) and Cu recovery (62.20%) performances in 60 min operation.
(c)
During the actual Cu-laden mine wastewater treatment process, over 98% inorganic salt was removed by the inorganic salt removal unit. Therefore, compared to the PECS, both the COD degradation and Cu recovery efficiencies were improved in MPECS, reaching 44.99% and 63.50%, respectively.

Jun Cui and Donghai Yuan provided strategy and advice for the research. Yinjie Zhang, Chang You, and Meng Ren performed the experiments. Meng Liu, Xin Xu, Yu Zhang contributed to analyze the data. Jun Cui, Aijun Lin, and Yuansheng Pei revised the manuscript.
